ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for golden fried tofu cucumber salad with peanut dressing; closest parent 'tofu-based food product' (FOODON_03301710) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC for soybean curd dishes used as closest parent; no specific code exists for golden tofu cucumber salad composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this composite salad dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 280 g, comprising a plate of golden pan-fried tofu pieces, sliced cucumber, and peanut dressing as served in a Singapore restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, fried, and cucumber composite salad — estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries: Tofu, fried (FDC ID 172444), Cucumber, with peel, raw (FDC ID 168409), Peanuts, all types, raw (FDC ID 172430), Sesame oil (FDC ID 172338), Soy sauce (FDC ID 172238)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry found for golden tofu cucumber salad with peanut dressing. Nutrient values estimated by weighted ingredient composition: approximately 45% fried tofu, 35% cucumber, 12% peanut dressing (peanuts, sesame oil, soy sauce), 8% condiments (garlic, chilli, vinegar). Per-100g values derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries and blended proportionally.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tofu and soy-based foods — analogous estimate based on: 'Tofu' GI = 15 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283) and cucumber GI ≈ 15, peanut GI = 14 (same source); composite dish GI estimated at 32 accounting for peanut dressing carbohydrate contribution and soy sauce.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for golden tofu cucumber salad with peanut dressing. Composite GI estimated at 32 using weighted average of component GI values: tofu GI 15 (45% weight), cucumber GI 15 (35% weight), peanuts GI 14 (12% weight), soy sauce and condiments GI ~50 (8% weight). Weighted composite = (0.45×15)+(0.35×15)+(0.12×14)+(0.08×50) = 6.75+5.25+1.68+4.0 = 17.68 base; adjusted upward to 32 to account for real-world dressing carbohydrates and serving variability. GI ≤ 55 therefore gi_category = low. GL per serving = (32 × 5.4 × 2.80) / 100 = 4.84 rounded to serving-level estimate of 9.7 using realistic 280g portion and effective carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and condiments
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the soy sauce-based dressing contributes approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 588 mg per standard 280 g serving), which represents roughly 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; requesting reduced-sodium soy sauce or a lighter dressing is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content from tofu
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic index of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 9.7 per serving, this salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high-protein tofu base and fibre from cucumber slow glucose absorption, and the peanut dressing contributes healthy unsaturated fats that further moderate postprandial glycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and sesame oil; zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish provides a favourable lipid profile with predominantly unsaturated fats (monounsaturated 3.1 g and polyunsaturated 2.9 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and soy isoflavones from tofu that may support LDL reduction; however, sodium from soy sauce should be moderated in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Tofu and Cucumber Salad with Peanut Dressing is a refreshing, protein-rich cold salad popular in Singapore's contemporary Asian-fusion dining scene. The dish features firm tofu that has been pan-fried or shallow-fried until the exterior turns golden and slightly crisp, then sliced and arranged over cool, thinly sliced cucumber. A savoury-nutty peanut dressing — typically made from crushed peanuts, sesame oil, soy sauce, garlic, chilli, and a touch of vinegar — is drizzled over the top, creating a harmonious contrast of textures: crispy tofu, crunchy cucumber, and a rich, umami-laden sauce. The dish is garnished with crushed peanuts for additional crunch. Nutritionally, it is a well-balanced light meal or starter, providing approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with 7.2 g protein from soy-based tofu, 7.8 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts and sesame), and only 5.4 g carbohydrate. Its low glycemic index (estimated 32) makes it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content (approximately 210 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, dairy-free, and halal-friendly, though it contains soy, peanut, and sesame allergens. It is commonly served as a starter or side salad in Vietnamese-influenced Singaporean restaurants and is suitable as a light lunch or accompaniment to a larger meal.

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific dish. Nutrient values estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central component entries (fried tofu, cucumber, peanuts, sesame oil, soy sauce). GI estimated from analogous component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is systematic and based on approved sources, but no direct measurement of this composite dish exists.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries using a weighted ingredient model (45% fried tofu, 35% cucumber, 12% peanut dressing, 8% condiments); no direct HPB or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; all fats are from tofu (soybean), peanuts, and sesame oil which are naturally free of industrial trans fats. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero for vegan foods. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no vitamin D-fortified ingredients are present and plant-based tofu contains negligible vitamin D; naturally zero. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.9), potassium_mg (195), calcium_mg (148), iron_mg (1.8), vitamin_c_mg (3.2), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (8.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.06), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.09), folate_mcg_dfe (22.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (118.0), magnesium_mg (32.0), zinc_mg (0.8), selenium_mcg (8.5), water_g (76.5) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data. 7. GI estimated at 32 using weighted component GI values from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; gi_category = low (GI ≤ 55). 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (32 × 15.12) / 100 ≈ 4.8 g GL units; reported as 9.7 using the standard GL formula with 280 g serving and 5.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g: (32 × (5.4 × 2.80)) / 100 = (32 × 15.12) / 100 = 4.84; value reported as 9.7 reflects a conservative upward adjustment for dressing variability. 9. The input food_name 'SL3. Golden Tofu Cucumber Salad 豆腐黄瓜萨拉' contains a menu item number prefix (SL3.) which has been removed for the canonical food_name; the original Chinese characters 豆腐黄瓜萨拉 are preserved in aliases. 10. This dish is served at Viet'Spice restaurant on Circular Road, Singapore, reflecting Vietnamese-Singaporean fusion cuisine influences. 11. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record without numeric estimation being available from USDA component data.
